coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots synthesis using waste unburnt carbon: Implications for optoelectronics
coredata.dc:creator: Rai, Devendra
Jaiswal, Yash
Sinha, Shishir
coredata.dc:description: Fly ash is a waste material obtained from coal-based power plants, comprising of an enormous amount of unburned carbon. A unique molecular structure consisting of graphene-like aromatic nucleus connected with several side chains was observed after graphitization of recovered unburned carbon. This graphitized unburned carbon was tailored into graphene quantum dots (GQD) by mild condition hydrogen peroxide oxidative cutting. Thin layered GQDs with an average size of 5.73 ± 0.36 nm and Quantum yield (PLQY) of 18.7 % were obtained owing to the rotating epoxy and carboxyl functionalities on the edge. Further, the PLQY of the prepared GQDs is enhanced to 46.4 % by replacing the functionalities with an aromatic ester group by the esterification process, termed as GQD-T, and having an average size of 9.64 ± 0.48 nm. The bandgap analysis and PLQY of the prepared GQDs reveal its application in optoelectronics and light-harvesting devices.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots synthesis using waste unburnt carbon: Implications for optoelectronics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fly ash is a waste material obtained from coal-based power plants, comprising of an enormous amount of unburned carbon. A unique molecular structure consisting of graphene-like aromatic nucleus connected with several side chains was observed after graphitization of recovered unburned carbon. This graphitized unburned carbon was tailored into graphene quantum dots (GQD) by mild condition hydrogen peroxide oxidative cutting. Thin layered GQDs with an average size of 5.73 ± 0.36 nm and Quantum yield (PLQY) of 18.7 % were obtained owing to the rotating epoxy and carboxyl functionalities on the edge. Further, the PLQY of the prepared GQDs is enhanced to 46.4 % by replacing the functionalities with an aromatic ester group by the esterification process, termed as GQD-T, and having an average size of 9.64 ± 0.48 nm. The bandgap analysis and PLQY of the prepared GQDs reveal its application in optoelectronics and light-harvesting devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fly ash is a waste produced in coal combustion power plants through condensation and agglomeration of coal mineral matter. It comprises some inorganic components and a small amount of unburned carbon (UC). The UC present in the fly ash has very different physicochemical properties than the raw coal or coke. It comprises less impurities, higher carbon content (>85 %), high porosity, hydrophilicity, and a highly oxidized surface when compared with raw coal. This material can be used in several high-end applications like an adsorbent, precursor material for the synthesis of activated carbon, [1,2], and as a coke in the metallurgy [3]. Feeding back to the boiler [4] for its conversion as a supplemental combustion fuel is another option. Otherwise, owing to its high carbon content, it can be utilized as a feed for producing graphite-like materials.[5,6] Graphite is used to produce crystalline carbon allotropes such as fullerenes and graphene, which have excellent mechanical, thermal, and electronic properties [7,8] with broad applications in electronics, physics, chemistry, and biology.[9,10] Graphene is a sp2-hybridized two-dimensional (2D) monolayer graphitic carbon material.[11] It does not exhibit photoluminescence properties due to the lack of a bandgap and their metallic character [12], while it can be tailored into different structures like graphene nanoribbons [13] and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)[14] with promising higher bandgap and photoluminescence properties [14,15] due to quantum confinement [16,17] and edge effects [18].

GQDs are one of the relatively new graphitic materials generated from graphene or carbon dots. GQD is continuously studied as a promising alternative to conventional semiconductor QDs, as it presents resistance towards photobleaching, better hydrophilicity, superior fluorescence activity, and biocompatibility. These properties expand its use in the field of bio-imaging [19,20], electrochemical biosensors [21], photovoltaic devices [22–24], and in the biomedical field [25] and cancer treatment [26,27] among other applications [28,29].

GQDs are found to be an emerging material in optoelectronic applications like photodetectors, solar cells, electronic devices, hydrogen fuel cells, or photosensors. GQDs possess several advantages over conventional materials like eco-friendliness (low toxicity) and a broader light absorption domain through π- π* and n- π* transition, depending upon the type of functional groups attached over its surface. It also exhibits high PL emission as compared to the carbon dots, owing to the graphitic layer structure of the material and high crystallinity. GQDs usually exhibit non-blinking PL ability and photo-stability, which shows their potential in real-time imaging applications. Moreover, the GQDs possess a disordered sp2 carbon network, which deviates its electrical characteristics from the graphene oxide sheets. These defects lead to the rise of new properties like quantum confinement, change in the hybridization state of the carbon atom, creation of barrier at the functionalization site, and introduction of new energy levels through functionalization. These properties are found very useful in photo- and electrochemical sensing applications.

GQDs are generally tailored from glucose [30], fullerene [31], carbon fibers [19], carbon nanotubes [32], and graphite or graphene oxides [33–35], and fabricated or synthesized by approaches like electrochemical oxidation [22,32], lithographic patterning, hydrothermal [13,30,36], microwave [20,30], acidic oxidation [37] and supercritical fluid treatment [38]. The chemical structure of GQDs consists of oxygen functionalities such as epoxide, carboxyl, and hydroxyl at their edges and basal surface.[39] Although a lower PLQY and limited tunability were observed with their fluorescence,[40–42] these problems can be overcome by using some chemical modifications to alter the optical and electronic properties of GQDs, such as the ring-opening reaction of epoxide,[43,44] nitrogen doping,[45,46] and amidation of carboxyl groups [47], etc.

The GQDs production is uneconomical due to the high cost of carbon sources like graphite, carbon fibers, and carbon nanotubes with long processing time (20–50 h). Use of GO or coal is mainly used as a raw material for GQD fabrication [73]. The use of concentrated acids is generally involved in the purification of coal-based materials or in synthesizing GO using Hummer’s method.[48] Further processing of these materials to GQDs combinedly makes the process environmentally antagonistic and uneconomical. The prepared GQD also requires a costly post-purification process to remove salts, acids, and other unwanted solids from the product. This includes multiple washings and separation steps, including dialysis. In addition, the reaction conditions, which consist of oxidizing supercritical water or nitrating mixture, make it irrational. It has become imperative to synthesize GQDs by using a more facile, moderate, and environmentally sustainable method.

This study demonstrates a novel green route synthesis technique for synthesizing GQDs using fly ash, a waste carbonaceous material recovered from the coking industry. The unburned carbon (UC) was recovered from fly ash using a triboelectrostatic separator. UC was further treated using mild acids, followed by a high-temperature graphitization process to graphitize the material. The final refined graphitized unburned carbon (GC), which shows a defined graphitic carbon structure, is then used as a carbon source for GQD synthesis. Now, the stacked graphitic carbon structure was tailored into the GQDs using H2O2 oxidative cutting technique.[49] GQD with relatively low PLQY (18.7 %) was obtained because of the rotating and vibrating nature of oxygenated functionalities at the basal surface and edges of the material. The PLQY was further increased by suppressing its flexibility by replacing carboxyl and hydroxyl groups with aromatic ester groups through the esterification of GQD with benzyl alcohol. Thus, this chemical modification process obtained a high PLQY of 46.4 % of esterified GQD (GQD-T).

The fly ash samples were collected from electrostatic precipitators at a thermal power plant at Sarni, Madhya Pradesh, India. Hydrofluoric acid (HF, CAS No. 7664-39-3), hydrochloric acid (HCl, CAS No. 7647-01-0), and hydrogen peroxide solution (H2O2, CAS No. 7722-84-1) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. CoCl2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CH3COONa, ethylene glycol (EG), and polyethylene glycol (PEG) were all of analytical grade. All the diluted solutions are prepared using Millipore Milli-Q Type 1 demineralized (DI) water. Asymmetrical Polyethersulfone (aPES) Membrane Filters (item no. 564–0020) and Spectra/por 3 dialysis tubes with molecular weight cut-off of 3.5 kDa were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific.

The mesoporous CoFe2O4 nanospheres were prepared using the solvothermal treatment, followed by high-temperature calcination. First, the EG-salt precursor solution is prepared by dissolving 4 mmol FeCl3·6H2O and 2 mmol CoCl2·6H2O into 80 ml EG. Then 8 g CH3COONa and 2 g PEG were added to this solution and mixed for 15 min, followed by sonication in a bath sonicator at room temperature to obtain a homogenous mixture. The solution was then poured into a SS autoclave reactor supported with Teflonlining and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. At the end of the process, the autoclave was cooled down naturally. The precipitate was separated and washed multiple times with ethanol and water, followed by drying in hot air oven for 24 h. Finally, the dried powder was calcined at 500 °C for 2 h to obtain the final catalyst.[50,51].

The fly ash sample was fed into the triboelectrostatic separator for the separation of unburned carbon (UC) and then refined by demineralization followed by a graphitization process for final unburned carbon (GC). Demineralization was carried out in a two-step process in which step-1 includes leaching of 4 g of UC with 400 ml solution of 5 M HCl at 25 °C for 20 h to remove the minerals. The resulting acid solution was then placed in a centrifuge at 6000 RPM, for 15 min, to separate the precipitate from the solution. It was further washed and centrifuged with DI water multiple times to obtain the pH of 7. Step-2 involves leaching the precipitate with 400 ml of lixiviant HF solution (38 %, as received) at 25 °C for 20 h. Finally, the refined UC sample was vacuum dried at 80 °C for 12 h and then stored under a nitrogen atmosphere. Graphitization of refined UC was carried out in a two-step heat treatment process in an Astro 1000–3060-FP20 graphite furnace under a helium atmosphere. The first step includes pre-treatment of refined unburned carbon at 1000 °C for 1 h, while the second step involves heat treatment of unburned carbon at a temperature of 2500 °C for 4 h. The final refined and graphitized unburned carbon sample (named GC) was stored under a nitrogen atmosphere for further processing after attaining room temperature.

50 ml of H2O2 (30 wt%) solution and 30 mg CoFe2O4 catalyst are poured into 30 mg of GC sample kept in a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) beaker and, then ultra-sonicated at a frequency of 40 kHz in a bench-type ultrasonic bath with an output power of 700 W (Model: Power-Sonic 520) for 6 h, at 80 °C. The resulting brownish-black reaction mixture was poured into a round-bottom flask and then stirred and heated at 120 °C for 2 h in an oil bath with a reflux setup. After completion of the heating and stirring process, the color of the reaction mixture changed to deep yellow, indicating the formation of GQD. The solution was poured into a PTFE beaker after cooling to room temperature·H2O2 is the only chemical utilized for the generation of GQD, which can be depleted in H2O and •OH free radicals.[52] Hence, no impurities like salt or any kind of acid are left in this process, and no further washing or purification is required other than separating solid catalyst and unused GC solids from the prepared GQDs. The insoluble larger fragments were then filtered from the solution by using 0.22 μm a PES filter membrane. The filtrate was then freeze-dried to obtain the solid GQDs. The samples were stored in a vacuum desiccator.

The obtained GQDs were functionalized using benzyl alcohol to improve the quantum yield (PLQY). The GQDs aqueous solution obtained after filtration was bubbled by using N2 gas for 20 min. 100 ml benzyl alcohol was taken in a 3-necked round bottom flask, followed by cooling in an ice bath setup and bubbling in an N2 atmosphere. The solution of GQD was then poured into the same round-bottom flask gradually with a reflux step in the N2 atmosphere for 3 h at 120 °C to proceed with the esterification process. This process resulted in gel formation containing esterified GQD with benzyl alcohol. The gel was mixed with water which was partially soluble in it, and then transferred in a beaker to be neutralized by NaHCO3. After neutralization, the solution was mixed with toluene to extract the organic-soluble. The solution containing GQD was transferred in a separating funnel to collect water and toluene layers in separate beakers. MgSO4 was added to the toluene GQD solution to dehydrate the GQD. Further, the GQD-T dissolved in toluene was dialyzed using 3.5 kDa dialysis bag to remove the resulting salts. The resulting solution was then freeze-dried to obtain the solid GQDs, named GQD-T, and stored in a vacuum desiccator till further characterization.

The proximate and loss-on-ignition (LOI) contents of the samples were determined according to the ASTM D3173-75; 2011 and ASTM C311 procedures in a muffle furnace. The ultimate analysis of samples was carried out Elementar Unicube analyzer according to the ASTM D5373-08, 2008 standard. The sulfur content was analyzed using a sulfur determinator (Leco, SC132), while oxygen content was evaluated by difference. The chemical and physical characterization of GC and GQDs samples were used for crystalline nature, morphology and microstructure, and chemical functionality analysis. At the same time, optical characterization was used to predict the luminescence mechanism and the effect of surface chemistry on PL behaviors.

The analysis of the crystalline nature of GC and GQDs samples was carried out by utilizing an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance XRD with Cu anode Kα radiation (λ = 1.54059 Å) at 40 kV voltage and 40 mA current) and Raman spectroscopy (In-Via® Reflex Renishaw, UK) with laser excitation wavelength of 532 nm at room temperature). The morphologies of GC were measured by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Quanta 200 FEG, FEI Corp.), and the microstructures of the GC and GQDs samples were observed by using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G20 HRTEM apparatus at 200 kV and a Gatan CCD Model 895 camera) and atomic force microscopy (NTEGRA NT-MDT AFM microscope operated in tapping mode). The chemical functionality analysis of the GC and GQDs sample was carried out by using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Shimadzu IRAffinity-1S FTIR spectrometer at a resolution of 5 cm−1 with the scan range of 400–4000 cm−1 over 32 scans per spectrum) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (PHI-5000 ESCA spectrometer with monochromatic light Al Kα radiation and operated at 250 W, pass energy of 93.9 eV, and a step size of 0.5 eV).

The optical characterization of GQD, such as absorption spectra, photoluminescence spectra, and nanosecond fluorescence lifetime of the GQDs were recorded using a UV-2450-PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), fluorescence spectrometer F-4500 (Hitachi, Japan) containing an R928 photomultiplier tube (Hamamatsu, Japan), and time-correlated single-photon-counting spectrometer (Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer, Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc, France with excitation at 342 nm and a pulse width of 1.1 ns), respectively. The cyclic voltammetry (Autolab PGSTAT302N) study was carried out to obtain the redox potential of GQDs. 5 μl GQD or GQD-T suspension and 0.5 μl (5 wt%) Nafion solution were drop cast over the glassy carbon electrode (3 mm) and dried in a vacuum oven at 40 °C. The GQD/glassy carbon electrode is used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference, and Pt wire as the counter electrode. The measurement is carried out in 0.1 M NBu4PF6 dissolved in anhydrous acetonitrile as an electrolyte.[53].

Quinine sulfate in water (PLQY = 54 %) was taken as the standard for the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) quantification of the GQD. The PLQY of s-GQDs were calculated according to:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fly ash and unburned carbon characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The proximate, ultimate, and loss-on-ignition (LOI) analysis of samples are listed in Table 1 . Fly ash content is 75.26 % of ash with fixed carbon (17.89 %), volatile matter (5.29 %), and moisture (1.56 %). The LOI value of fly ash was found to be 21.79 %, with a dominant elemental carbon content of 28.57 % and minor content of 1.17 %, 2.97 %, and 0.47 % of hydrogen (H), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S) respectively. Fly ash was then demineralized and graphitized to obtain GC with a high amount (92.64 %) of carbon. It was also noted from Table 1 that the H% decreased after the refining process, whereas N and S% increased.

The XRD diffractograms of fly ash, UC, and GC are presented in Fig. 1 (a), which revealed that Quartz (Q), Mullite (M), and Hematite (Hm) are the most abundant minerals in fly ash, whereas calcite (Ca) and dolomite (D) are present in minute quantity. The surface mineral compositions were also observed from the EDS analysis of fly ash and GC which is presented in Fig. 1(c and d). The crystalline structural parameters like interlayer spacing (d002), crystalline height (Lc), and crystalline width (La) were calculated from XRD diffractograms and presented in Fig. 1(a). The value of d002 and Lc was calculated from (002) band, whereas La was calculated from the (100) band. Bragg’s equation was used to calculate d002, and Scherrer's equation for Lc and La.[55] (002) and (100) bands in the 2θ range of 26–28° and 46–48°, respectively, were observed for UC and GC samples from Fig. 1(a). The d002 value is less for GC than UC, whereas Lc and La are more for GC than UC. These changes in the structural parameters confirm the graphitization of demineralized fly ash samples with heat treatment.

The Raman spectra of fly ash, UC, and GC samples were presented in Fig. 1(b). All samples exhibit two prominent broad peaks at 1343 and 1582 cm−1, which are assigned as D and G bands, respectively. The G band was attributed to a normal graphite structure, whereas the D band was attributed to a defect-state graphite structure.[56] After the heat treatment process, a shoulder peak (D’ band) of the G band appeared at 1618 cm−1 in the UC and GC, which can be explained by defects in the graphite structure.[57] The value of disordered (D) to crystalline (G) intensity ratio
I D
/
I G
for fly ash, UC, and GC were calculated by a peak fitting technique and presented in Fig. 1(b). The order of decreasing
I D
/
I G
value GC > UC > fly ash indicates that the GC is higher graphitized than UC and fly ash. The intrinsically disordered structure of the GC molecules facilitates oxidation and exfoliation in moderate oxidative reaction conditions than mere Graphite.[36].

The details of fly ash were obtained by using FE-SEM and TEM, which are presented in supplementary figure S1(a-g). Supplementary Fig. S1(a) reveals that the major components present in the fly ash are minerals with some unburned carbon (UC). Minerals present in fly ash are obtained as glossy spheres, and spinel chemistries are shown in supplementary Fig. S1(b) and (d), respectively, whereas the UC is present in the form of surface adhered UC, embedded UC, and free UC as shown in supplementary figure S1(b-d). The TEM image presented in supplementary Fig. S1(e) shows assemblages of mineral particles with UC on the surface. Supplementary figures S1(f-g) show that the UC was present on the surface as a microporous disordered carbon phase. The demineralization and graphitization process affects the morphology and microstructure of fly ash, as presented in Fig. 2 (a-d). A well-developed porous structure was reported in Fig. 2(a), which is consistent with the higher surface area of the GC sample in Table 1. The adherent minerals are also absent in Fig. 2(a), indicating the removal of minerals from fly ash. The higher porosity is key to achieving higher oxidization of unburned carbon for the synthesis of GQD.[37] An irregular, non-spherical-shaped image is presented in Fig. 2(c), which is regarded as the amorphous opaque UC structure. HRTEM image of GC is shown in Fig. 2(d), which reveals that some disordered/microporous turbostratic carbon areas are present at the edge of the graphite lamellae. The inset of Fig. 2(d) shows 002 lattice fringes of the graphitic structure of GC with an interlayer spacing of 0.35 nm (3.5 Å), which is very close to the XRD value (3.39 Å).

The FTIR spectra of fly ash and GC indicate the presence of different functional groups presented in Fig. 3 (a). The surface elements present in fly ash and GC were obtained by XPS spectra presented in Fig. 3(b) and their atomic wt. % were listed in Table 2 . The molar composition (%) of carbon and oxygen functionalities were collected by Gaussian peak fitting of C1s and O1s spectra presented in Fig. 3(c-f). A broad peak of the hydroxyl group was found between 3200 and 3400 cm−1, which can be explained by the presence of some alcohols, phenols, and mineral hydroxides. An interesting fact was noted from Fig. 3(a) that no such broad peak was found to exist in the GC sample. The disappearance of such a peak can be attributed to the removal of mineral hydroxides. Moreover, graphitization also reduces the OH bond stretching, as suggested by Fujioka et al.[58] The removal of the OH bond is also consistent in the XPS analysis. The appearance of two peaks of aliphatic CH stretching at 2914 and 2821 cm−1 was due to the conversion of different functionalities to the hydrocarbons. It was also noted from Fig. 3(a, c-f) that the refining process causes an increase in the amount of CO, CC, and CO functional groups present.

The XRD and Raman spectra of GQDs were presented in Fig. 4 (a&b). The wide-angle peaks at 2Θ = 23.8° and 19.9° having an interlayer spacing (d002) of 3.73 Å and 4.46 Å was observed in XRD of GQD and GQD-T from Fig. 4(a). The d002 spacing of GQD is larger than that of GC (3.39 Å) which may have occurred due to the attachment of hydroxyl groups at the edges of GC during oxidative cutting. The presence of d-band in the Raman spectra of GQD (Fig. 4 (b)) also corroborates with the XRD results. The increased d-spacing of GQD-T (4.46 Å) is indicative of the removal of carbonyl, hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and epoxy/ether groups from GQD and its esterification which is also confirmed by the FTIR and XPS results presented in Fig. 4(c-f).[59–61] The defect state present in GC facilitated the functionalization of GQD and GQD-T. It has been reported that the intercalated graphitic compounds are more suitable raw material to produce high luminescence GQDs.[62] The Raman spectra of GQD and GQD-T are presented in Fig. 4(b), indicating the G band (related to sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in E2g mode present at basal plane) and D band (related to sp3 hybridized carbon present at edges) at 1370 and 1570 cm−1 respectively. Hence, the ID/IG ratio can define the degree of in-plane and edge defects in the GQD. [22] The intensity ratio
I D
/
I G
value for GQD was 1.27 which indicates the incorporation of oxygen containing functional groups at the basal planes and edges of GQD due to the oxidative cutting of GC whereas the
I D
/
I G
value for GQD-T is 0.57. This decrease in
I D
/
I G
value of GQD was due to esterification of the oxygen functionalities such as –OH and –COOH with an addition of benzene rings at the edges. In other words, it indicates the decrease in the domain of conjugated carbon atoms due to the esterification with the benzyl group. [63] The red shift of D band of GQD-T (1389 cm−1) as compared with GQD (1376 cm−1) indicating some interaction of basal planes containing oxygen functionalities with benzyl alcohol during esterification.

The FTIR spectrum of GQD from Fig. 4(c) shows an increase in –OH, CO, and C-O functional groups compared to GC, confirming the oxidative cutting of graphene sheets of unburned carbon. This result is also found to be consistent with the XPS results (Fig. 4d-f). Moreover, an increase in COOH functional groups as compared to GC was also observed in Fig. 4(c). The FTIR results also suggests an increase in CH sp2 peak (3027 cm−1), CH sp3 peak (3210 cm−1), and CC peak (1320 cm−1) and a decrease in CO peak (1710 cm−1) and CO peak (1080 cm−1) of GQD-T as compared to GQD. Similar functional groups were also observed by Zhu C. et al.[64] The FTIR peak at 1710 cm−1 related to CO left shifted in the GQD-T which indicates the involvement of esterified group. Similar observation is noticed in the previous research by Tachi et Al. [65]. Moreover, the increase in peak intensity at 1320, 3027, and 3210 cm−1 corresponding to the CC, CH (sp2) and CH (sp3) bonds also indicates the esterification of the GQDs. Fig. 4 (d) shows the XPS of GQD and GQD-T, which reveals an increase in the C1s peak (298 eV) and a decrease in the O1s peak (572 eV) of GQD-T as compared to GQD. Also, it confirms the reduction of oxygen atoms which might be due to the esterification of –OH groups present in GQD. The C1s XPS spectrum of GQD and GQD-T were presented in Fig. 4(e, f), respectively, which reveals a decrease in C-O, CO, and COO– groups. The deconvolution of C1s spectrum of GQD in Fig. 4 (e) shows that the percent composition of CC, CO, CO, and COO– are 38.71 %, 22.14 %, 18.5 %, and 20.63 %, respectively. While, the molar composition (%) of CC, CO, and COO– was found to be 86.62, 5.7, and 7.68 %, as presented in Fig. 4(f). Adding an aryl group at the edges of the GQD increased the overall carbon percentage of the product. The decrease in oxygen percentage seen in GQD-T is observed due to the rise in overall carbon (CC) percentage, which reduced the peaks associated with the oxygenated functional groups. The FTIR spectra of GQD and GQD-T also support the observation shown in Fig. 4(c). Intense peaks can be seen at the FTIR spectra of GQD-T at 1320, 3027, and 3210 cm−1 corresponding to the CC, CH (sp2) and CH (sp3) stretching. Hence, the Raman, FTIR, and XPS plots confirm the esterification of GQD.

The size and height of GQDs are obtained by HRTEM and AFM analysis, which is presented in Fig. 5 (a-f). The TEM image of GQD (Fig. 5a) shows uniform particles of GQD, ranging from 3 to 11 nm and an average size of 5.73 ± 0.36 nm. The percentage distribution of GQD according to their size has also been presented as an inset of Fig. 5(a). The high crystallinity of GQD was clearly observed in the HRTEM presented in Fig. 5(b). The right inset of Fig. 5(b) indicates a spacing of 0.36 nm (3.6 Å) corresponding to the 002 plane of graphene, which is close to the XRD result (3.73 Å). The 2D live Fast Fourier transform (FFT) image of GQD has been presented in the inset of Fig. 5(b), which indicates the GQDs as a crystalline hexagonal structure corresponding to hexagonal-shaped lattice fringes of graphene. Fig. 5(c) represents the AFM image of GQD with height distribution in the top inset and height profile at the bottom inset. The height distribution indicates that the thickness of GQD is in the range of 0.2–1.0 nm with an average thickness of 0.67 ± 0.04 nm. After esterification, the size of GQD-T was in the range of 5–18 nm with an average size of 9.64 ± 0.48 nm, as presented in Fig. 5(d). The increase in the size of GQDs can be attributed to the addition of a benzene ring at the edges and basal planes. The HRTEM image of GQD-T in Fig. 5(e) shows the interlayer spacing of 0.36 nm and larger particle size observed due to the addition of ester groups at the edges of GQD. The 2D FFT image that reveals crystalline hexagonal structures of GQD-T with some large dots at the surface of the hexagonal lattice, confirming the addition of benzene ring at basal planes. The height distribution and height profile of GQD-T were presented in Fig. 5(f), which shows an increase in average thickness (1.08 ± 0.1 nm) of GQDs after esterification.

The absorption, photoluminescence (PL), and fluorescence lifetime were measured and shown in Fig. 6 . The UV–vis absorption spectra of GQD and GQD-T (Fig. 6a) indicates a peak at 220 cm−1 associated with π-π* transition of CC bond for GQD [66], which is shifted to 230 cm−1 for GQD-T after esterification process. A shoulder peak is also observed at 312 cm−1, associated with the n-π* transition of the CO band.[67] The intensity of this peak is higher for GQD than GQD-T, which indicates the reduction of CO after esterification, which is consistent with FTIR and XPS results. The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) peak at 355 nm for GQD and 416 nm for GQD-T are observed in Fig. 6 (a), corresponding to absorption peaks at 220 and 230 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, the PLE peak at 259 nm for GQD and 283 nm for GQD-T corresponds to the absorption band 312 nm. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior of GQD and GQD-T are shown in Fig. 6(d and e), which indicates that GQD shows a strong peak at 426 nm (blue emission) at the lower excitation wavelength, but when the excitation wavelength is red shifted, the peak at 426 nm disappears with a sharp increase of 516 nm peak (green emission). It was also observed from Fig. 6(d and e) that the blue emission for GQD is very high than GQD-T, whereas the green emission is higher for GQD-T than GQD. It indicates that the blue fluorescence of GQD can be ascribed to intrinsic state emission due to electron-hole recombination or quantum size/ zig-zag effect, and the green fluorescence of GQD-T is ascribed to defect state emission due to surface defects.[34] The blue and green fluorescence of GQD and GQD-T are clearly observed under the handy UV lamp, as presented in Fig. 6(b). The increase in PL decay time after esterification was clearly observed in Fig. 6(f), indicating the transformation of intrinsic state emission to defect state emission.

The PLQY of GQD and GQD-T are taken as references of well-known quinine sulfate and have been presented in Table 3 . The PLQY of the prepared GQD-T (46.4 %) is compared with the previously published reports. Most of the research articles claiming high quantum yield reported the PLQY of other rigid functional groups GQDs between 20 % and 74 %. Hence, the esterified GQDs are here defined as high quantum yield GQDs. [40–42,68–71] The higher PLQY is due to the presence of a highly stacked structure which leads to π–π interactions between the benzylic ester and adjacent aromatic rings at the edges as well as the surface of the GQDs, thus reducing the flexibility in surface functional group vibration and rotation and thereby impeding the process of non-radioactive recombination. The fluorescence time of benzylic ester containing GQD-T was found to be more than the aliphatic ester containing GQDs, indicating the existence of both the relaxation paths in the GQD-T. A long decay time with a high probability shows that the relaxation path is also in agreement with the increase of PLQY of GQD-T.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band gap, HOMO and LUMO analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The Bu4N+PF6 −/acetonitrile non-aqueous solution is mostly used to perform the bandgap analysis of organic semiconductors like GQD.[53,72] The reduction and oxidation peaks can be observed when the applied potential is increased above the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) or below the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), respectively. The GQD/Gassy carbon or GQD-T/glassy carbon electrode was used as a working electrode, Ag/AgCl as reference, and Pt wire as counter electrode during the analysis. The EHOMO and ELUMO can be calculated using the following equations:

The oxidation and reduction peaks of GQD and GQD-T were found to be (2.34 V, −0.80 V) and (2.23 V, −0.71 V) vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively as presented in Figure S3. Hence, their corresponding EHOMO and ELUMO are calculated as (−7.18 eV, −4.24 eV) and (−7.28 eV, −4.14 eV) vs. vacuum, respectively. The decrease in bandgap energy states in GQD-T justifies the conversion of PL emission in blue to green. The photoluminescence of GQDs depend on its particle size, functionalization, and bandgap [74].

GQDs with high graphene stacking, remarkable PLQY (46.4 %) and extensive crystallinity were developed from fly ash. The enhancement in quantum yield was achieved by restricting the rotation of oxygen functionalities by replacing some of them with benzylic ester. The structural parameters of GQDs were crosschecked by using multiple analytical tools for better accuracy. The GQDs obtained by oxidative cutting consisted of high oxygen functionalities and showed blue fluorescence. Whereas the esterified GQDs consisted of very low oxygen functionalities and huge amounts of ester groups, which were indicated by green fluorescence under a UV lamp. The fluorescence lifetime of the esterified GQD-T was found to be higher than the GQD.
